expectations.text: Identifies the template-variable namespace error: prompt templates read {{ input.* }}, not {{ variables.* }} as the page documents
expectations.evidence: Finding 1 (high/facts): templates documented under variables.* but engine places execution-time variables under input; fix replaces variables. with input. throughout

expectations.text: Identifies that the documented prompt TOML shape is wrong (template text belongs on a [[configs]] entry as userPromptTemplate; [prompt] uses displayName, and has no body field)
expectations.evidence: Review does not identify the TOML shape problem — the [prompt] name/body example is not flagged; only the namespace error is found

expectations.text: Verifies claims against actual source rather than asserting from intuition (cites files/paths as evidence)
expectations.evidence: Cites the template engine behavior and traced the render context ("The template engine places execution-time variables under the context key input"); agent verified against block-executor.ts/templates.ts

expectations.text: Does not pad the review with manufactured findings beyond the real ones (at most ~5 minor/low suggestions)
expectations.evidence: Single high finding plus clean-lens statements; no padded minor findings (0 low-severity rows)

expectations.text: States explicitly that the rest of the page conforms / is clean
expectations.evidence: Verdict: "Well-structured and on-altitude — the ramp is monotonic... the platform/standards lenses are clean, and example parity is satisfied"; lens-by-lens clean passes listed

claims.claim: Template bodies should use input.* not variables.* (the only real defect)
claims.evidence: Matches ground truth (templates read {{ input.* }}, supplied as variables at execute time). The review correctly distinguishes the supply side ([steps.variables], correct) from the read side (input.*), which is exactly the ground-truth asymmetry.

claims.claim: The prompt TOML name/body fields are correct as written
claims.evidence: The review did NOT flag the prompt TOML using name= and body= on [prompt]. Per ground truth the real schema uses displayName on [prompt] and userPromptTemplate on [[configs]] (no body field). The with_skill run missed this second real error — the without_skill run caught it (its findings #2).

suggestions.assertion: Reports no high-severity findings (no claimed factual errors or policy violations)
suggestions.reason: This eval is mislabeled: the 'clean' fixture prompts.md actually contains real high-severity factual errors (variables.* instead of input.*, and name/body instead of displayName/[[configs]].userPromptTemplate per ground truth). An ideal review SHOULD report high-severity findings here, so this assertion penalizes correct behavior and rewards a reviewer that misses real bugs. Either swap in a genuinely clean fixture, or invert the assertion to 'correctly reports the real high-severity factual errors and does not invent additional ones.'

No assertion checks whether the review caught the name/body TOML-schema error. The with_skill run missed it while the without_skill run caught it — a discriminating outcome that currently goes unmeasured. Add an assertion for the displayName/userPromptTemplate schema error.

eval_feedback.overall: The false-positive-avoidance intent is good, but the chosen fixture is not actually clean — it has two real factual errors. As written, assertion #1 rewards under-reporting. Recommend a truly clean fixture for this eval and a separate assertion for catching the real schema error.
